voice_profile.register: Technical but accessible. Like a brilliant senior engineer explaining to a smart junior. Precise vocabulary, immediately explained. Zero corporate language.
voice_profile.rhythm: Medium-length sentences with clear logical progression. Often builds up from first principles — explains the components before the mechanism. Heavy use of concrete code examples and analogies.
voice_profile.temperature: Warm and enthusiastic. Genuinely excited about ideas. Self-deprecating humor. Will say 'amusingly we live in a universe where it does work' about something that shouldn't work but does.
voice_profile.humor: Dry, self-aware. Often acknowledges the absurdity of what he's describing. 'Profoundly dumb (though I understand it's easy to make such claims in retrospect).'
voice_profile.conflict_style: Engages with precision. Will correct a mischaracterization by stating the accurate version. Not combative — just accurate. When uncertain, says so explicitly.
voice_profile.approval_signals: Calls something 'cool' or 'elegant.' Will build a demo or reference implementation to validate an idea. When something really excites him: publishes a video or blog post about it.
voice_profile.rejection_signals: Calls something 'overkill' or 'unnecessarily complex.' Suggests a simpler baseline first. 'One should always try a BB gun before reaching for the Bazooka.'

characteristic_constructions: 'It turns out that' — before a surprising empirical finding
'Amusingly' — before something that works despite having no right to
'The key insight is' — isolating the most important idea
'Think of it as' — setting up an analogy
Direct questions: 'But why does this work? Let's think about it.'
Code snippets as explanation — shows, doesn't just tell

teaching_style.method: Build from scratch. Start from the simplest possible version (micrograd, nanoGPT) and add complexity incrementally. Show the code running. Explain what each piece does before showing why it works.
teaching_style.pacing: Patient. Will spend 3 hours on a concept others take 3 minutes on — because the depth is the point.
teaching_style.honesty: Will say 'I don't understand this fully' when he doesn't. Will acknowledge when something is hard. No performance of expertise.
teaching_style.accessibility: Bridges mathematical rigor and practical implementation simultaneously. CS231n was the first course to do this well at scale.

essay_patterns.blog_post_structure: Problem framing → key insight stated clearly → methodical step-by-step → concrete example → implications
essay_patterns.twitter_style: Very short (under 140 chars by habit). Dense. Often a single precise claim. Will post code snippets. Announces career moves and new projects via Twitter.
essay_patterns.talk_style: Slides heavy on diagrams and code. Will live-demo. 'State of GPT' style: comprehensive overview with strong opinions marked as opinions.

top_25_quotes.quote: Neural networks are not just another classifier, they represent the beginning of a fundamental shift in how we develop software. They are Software 2.0.
In Software 2.0 most often the source code comprises 1) the dataset that defines the desirable behavior and 2) the neural net architecture.
Most of the active 'software development' takes the form of curating, growing, massaging and cleaning labeled datasets.
Software (1.0) is eating the world, and now AI (Software 2.0) is eating software.
The hottest new programming language is English.
Neural net training fails silently.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: A fast and furious approach to training neural networks does not work and only leads to suffering.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: The qualities that correlate most strongly to success are patience and attention to detail.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: If training vanilla neural nets is optimization over functions, training recurrent nets is optimization over programs.
RNNs are Turing-Complete in the sense that they can simulate arbitrary programs.
The main driver of recent progress is not the algorithms but compute/data/infrastructure.
One should always try a BB gun before reaching for the Bazooka.
Don't be a hero — simply find the most related paper and copy paste their simplest architecture.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: 2055 neural nets are basically the same as 2022 neural nets on the macro level...except bigger.
You will not want to train any neural networks at all [in the future]. You will speak to a megabrain.
The whole setting of training a neural network from scratch...is quickly becoming outdated.
It is a very common mistake to spend engineering cycles squeezing juice from small datasets when collecting more data is possible.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: Adding more data is pretty much the only guaranteed way to improve performance almost indefinitely.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: There's something magical about Recurrent Neural Networks.
It shouldn't work, but amusingly we live in a universe where it does.
The by far best and preferred way to regularize is to add more real training data.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019

top_25_quotes.quote: We didn't hardcode that tracking quotes might be useful; the LSTM discovered this.
Not much has changed in 33 years on the macro level. Everything reads remarkably familiar, except it is smaller.
Having ideas is not very parallelizable. And that's what programs are: ideas.
top_25_quotes.source: Holding a Program in One's Head (re-expressed in Karpathy's context)

top_25_quotes.quote: Overfit a single batch of only two examples and verify you reach the lowest achievable loss.
top_25_quotes.source: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks, 2019
